text: The surgeon performs a trans-cystic exploration and visually identifies a stone within the cystic duct.
The surgeon opens the common bile duct to perform a choledochotomy, preparing to access the duct for stone removal.
The surgeon uses a choledochoscope to visualize the common bile duct, as the stone is not floating within the duct.
The surgeon deploys a basket behind a large stone within the common bile duct and carefully drags the stone upwards, utilizing the basket's mechanism to maneuver the stone towards the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a basket to drag the stone upwards, positioning the basket behind the stone to facilitate its movement.
The stone visibly breaks into fragments as the surgeon manipulates it with the basket.
The surgeon removes stone fragments from the surgical field, using instruments to carefully extract each piece.
The surgeon irrigates the common bile duct, flushing out remaining stone fragments and debris from the duct.
After irrigating the common bile duct, the surgeon inserts the choledochoscope to visualize and identify stone fragments that have migrated distally within the duct.
The surgeon uses a colodocuscope to inspect the distal portion of the common bile duct after ensuring the proximal area is clear.
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to grasp and remove stones from the common bile duct. Following the removal, the duct is closed using interrupted sutures with 5-0 PDS material.
The surgeon performs a completion cholangiogram through the cystic duct opening, ensuring visualization of the bile ducts despite previous scoping procedures.
